text: Scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy and extraspondyric dissection with perineal extraction of the specimen for cancer of anorectal junction arising on the background of long-standing ulcerative colitis. A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms. This found largely normal mucosa but a fibropithelial polyp at the anorectal junction. No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy. Histology from the biopsy showed high-grade dysplasia with some features raising the possibility of deeper invasion. There was no evidence of metastatic disease on staging CT. MRI rectum could not visualize the primary tumor and gave overall TNM-A stage DXN-M-M. After discussion and multidisciplinary meeting, the patient proceeded to surgery.

Scarless, entirely laparoscopic panproctolectomy and extraspondyric dissection with perineal extraction of the specimen for cancer of anorectal junction arising on the background of long-standing ulcerative colitis. A sixty-six-year-old male with a background history of long-standing ulcerative colitis underwent flexible sigmoidoscopy for a flare-up of symptoms. This found largely normal mucosa but a fibropithelial polyp at the anorectal junction. No synchronous lesions were detected on colonoscopy. Histology from the biopsy showed high-grade dysplasia with some features raising the possibility of deeper invasion. There was no evidence of metastatic disease on staging CT. MRI rectum could not visualize the primary tumor and gave overall TNM-A stage DXN-M-M. After discussion and multidisciplinary meeting, the patient proceeded to surgery.

The surgeon uses five laparoscopic ports to perform the colectomy, with three 12mm ports in the umbilical region, right iliac fossa, and left hypochondrium, and two 5mm ports in the right hypochondrium and left iliac fossa. The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient to mobilize the right colon, performing medial to lateral dissection of the right colonic mesentery. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, clipped, and divided using hemolog clips. The terminal ileal mesentery is divided, allowing the terminal ileum to be divided with a stapler. The surgeon then moves caudally to access the transverse colon, dividing the gastrocolic ligament to enter the lesser sac and mobilize the transverse colon. The mesentery of the transverse colon is dissected to approach the middle colic vessels, with multiple lymphatic and venous branches divided, followed by ligation and division of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon uses five laparoscopic ports to perform the operation, with three 12mm ports in the umbilical region, right iliac fossa, and left hypochondrium, and two 5mm ports in the right hypochondrium and left iliac fossa. The surgeon stands on the left side of the patient to mobilize the right colon using the left hypochondrium and left iliac fossa ports. After establishing pneumoperitoneum, the right colonic mesentery is dissected from medial to lateral. The ileocolic pedicle is identified, clipped, and divided with hemolog clips at a mid-mesenteric level. The terminal ileal mesentery is divided, allowing the terminal ileum to be divided with a stapler. The surgeon then moves caudally to access the transverse colon, dividing the gastrocolic ligament to enter the lesser sac and mobilize the transverse colon. The mesentery of the transverse colon is dissected to approach the middle colic vessels, with multiple lymphatic and venous branches divided, followed by ligation and division of the middle colic artery.

The surgeon switches to the right side of the patient, using ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa to approach the splenic flexure. The left colon and rectum are mobilized with careful movements to avoid splenic injury. The inferior mesenteric artery is identified and high ligation is performed following a medial to lateral dissection. The rectum is mobilized, and the peritoneal reflection is divided, proceeding in the plane of total mesorectal excision to the middle to low rectal canal.

The surgeon switches to the right side of the patient, using ports in the right hypochondrium and iliac fossa to approach the splenic flexure. The left colon and rectum are mobilized with careful movements to avoid splenic injury. The inferior mesenteric artery is identified and high ligation is performed following a medial to lateral dissection. The laparoscopic procedure concludes with the mobilization of the rectum, dividing the peritoneal reflection and proceeding with total mesorectal excision to the middle to low rectal canal.

The laparoscopic view shows the perineal dissection being performed in a codocranial direction. The specimen is extracted through the perineal wound. The pelvic peritoneum is closed over a suction drain using an intracorporeal continuous suture.

The laparoscopic view shows the perineal dissection being performed. The specimen is extracted through the perineal wound. The pelvic peritoneum is then closed over a suction drain using an intracorporeal continuous suture.

The surgeon completes the operation by forming an ileostomy through the incision of one of the ports in the right iliac fossa. The visual content includes the creation of the ileostomy and the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon completes the operation by forming an ileostomy through the incision of one of the ports located in the right iliac fossa.
